coredata.dc:title: Facile preparation and characterization of new green emitting carbon dots for sensitive and selective off/on detection of Fe3+ ion and ascorbic acid in water and urine samples and intracellular imaging in living cells
coredata.dc:creator: Shamsipur, Mojtaba
Molaei, Karam
Molaabasi, Fatemeh
Alipour, Mohsen
Alizadeh, Naader
Hosseinkhani, Saman
Hosseini, Morteza
coredata.dc:description: Carbon dots (CDs) have gained great attention as multifunctional materials because of their interesting properties and general applicability. However, there are some reports for the preparation of highly luminescent green-emitting CDs (G-CDs), although these reports seem not to be extensible. Herein, new G-CDs (quantum yield: 27.2%) were synthesized from a facile hydrothermal treatment of p-aminosalicylic acid and ethylene glycol dimethacrylate as both carbon and nitrogen source and cross-linking agent, respectively. The chemical composition and optical properties of the as-prepared G-CDs were successfully investigated using transmission electron microscopy, atomic force microscopy, dynamic light scattering, X-ray diffraction, energy dispersive X-ray spectroscopy, Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy and fluorescence and UV–vis spectroscopies. Interestingly, the fluorescence intensity of G-CDs was selectivity quenched by Fe3+ in the range of 0.05–10.0 µmol Lˉ1, with a detection limit of 13.7 nmol Lˉ1. Meanwhile, ascorbic acid found to reduce Fe3+ to Fe2+, thereby causing restoration of the fluorescence of G-CDs. The detection limit for ascorbic acid detection was estimated as 82.0 nmol Lˉ1 over a linear range from 0.2 to 11.0 µmol Lˉ1. Furthermore, the designed sensing platform was successfully utilized to the detection of Fe3+ and ascorbic acid in water and urine samples and to intracellular imaging without surface modification.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Green-emitting carbon dots
Synthesis and characterization
Fe3+ ion
Ascorbic acid
Intracellular imaging

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Fluorescence detection of Fe3+ and ascorbic acid
Use of fluorescent G-CDs nanoprobes for detection of Fe3+ ions and ascorbic acid

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors gratefully acknowledge the support of this work by the Research Councils of Tarbiat Modares and Razi Universities, Iran Science Elites Federation ( ISEF ) and Iran National Science Foundation ( INSF ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) TEM image of G-CDs (a1) and B-CDs (a2), along with their size distribution histograms. (b) FT-IR spectrum of G-CDs.

(a) UV–vis absorption (black line), excitation (blue line) and emission (green line) spectra of G-CDs in dilute aqueous solutions. Inset shows the photograph of aqueous dispersion of G-CD under UV (365 nm). (b) Fluorescence emission spectra of G-CDs under different excitation wavelength (from 340 nm to 430 nm). (c) The influence of pH on the fluorescence intensity of G-CDs at emission of 520 nm. (d) Fluorescence spectra of G-CDs dissolved in water, methanol, acetonitrile and ethanol. Inset shows the photographs of different solvents before (top) and after (bottom) addition of G-CDs under UV (365 nm).

(a) Fluorescence emission spectra of the G-CDs (2.5 μg mL−1) aqueous solution in the presence of different Fe3+ concentrations (from top to bottom: 0.0, 0.05, 0.1, 0.5, 1.0, 1.25, 2.8, 4.5, 6.5, 10, 15, 20, 30, 40 and 50 µmol Lˉ1). (b) The linear relationship of F0/F versus concentrations of Fe3+ ions over the range of 0.05–10 μM. F and F0 are the FL intensities at 520 nm in the presence and absence of Fe3+ ions, respectively. (c) Selectivity of the G-CDs platform for Fe3+ detection. The concentrations are 2.5 µg mL−1 for G-CDs, 0.5 µmol Lˉ1 for Fe3+ and 50 µmol Lˉ1 for other different metal ions. (d) Fluorescence emission spectra of the G-CDs/Fe3+ platform (2.5 μg mL−1 of G-CDs and 50 µmol Lˉ1 of Fe3+) in the presence of increasing concentration of ascorbic acid (from bottom to top: .0, 0.2, 0.8, 2.2, 4.0, 6.5, 8.8, and 11.0 µmol Lˉ1). (e) The linear relationship of F/F0 versus the concentration of ascorbic acid over the range from 0.2 to 11.0 µmol Lˉ1; F0 and F are the fluorescence intensities of the G-CDs/Fe3+ platform at 520 nm in the absence and presence of ascorbic acid, respectively. (f) Selectivity of the G-CDs/Fe3+ platform toward to ascorbic acid (10 µmol Lˉ1), the concentration of the all ions and amino acids is 100 µmol Lˉ1. Error bars are the standard deviations of three replicate measurements.

(a) Time resolved decay curves of G-CDs in the absence (blue) and presence (pink) of 1.0 µM Fe3+. (b) Viability of BT474 cell after incubation with different concentrations of G-CDs for 24 h, as determined by a standard MTT assay. The error bars represent standard deviation of three independent experiments. Fluorescence microscopy images (c and f), and their corresponding bright field (d and g) and merged images (e and h) of BT474 cells: (c and d) incubated with 0.5 mg mL−1 G-CDs for 6 h at 37 °C, (f and g) first incubated with 0.5 mg mL−1 G-CDs for 6 h at 37 °C and then incubated with 0.8 µmol Lˉ1 FeCl3 for 1 h at 37 °C. Scale bar: 20 µm.
Schematic illustration of the formation process of G-CDs from PAS and EGDMA by hydrothermal synthesis and the Fe3+ and ascorbic acid detection mechanism using G-CDs.
Comparison of figures of merits of different types of fluorescent sensing platforms for the detection of Fe3+ and ascorbic acid with those obtained in present work.
Determination of Fe3+ in different water samples and recovery of ascorbic acid in human urine samples detected by the G-CDs/Fe3+ sensing platform (n= 3).

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: a
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: t-critical = 4.3 for degree of freedom 4 and P = 0.0.

head.ce:title: Facile preparation and characterization of new green emitting carbon dots for sensitive and selective off/on detection of Fe3+ ion and ascorbic acid in water and urine samples and intracellular imaging in living cells

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Razi University, Kermanshah, Iran

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Tarbiat Modares University, Tehran, Iran

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Biology, Tarbiat Modares University, Tehran, Iran

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Biology, Tarbiat Modares University

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Advanced Medical Sciences & Technologies, School of Medicine, Jahrom University of Medical Sciences, Jahrom, Iran

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Advanced Medical Sciences & Technologies, School of Medicine, Jahrom University of Medical Sciences

ce:affiliation.ce:label: e
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Life Science Engineering, Faculty of New Sciences & Technologies, Tehran University, Tehran, Iran

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Life Science Engineering, Faculty of New Sciences & Technologies, Tehran University

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbon dots (CDs) have gained great attention as multifunctional materials because of their interesting properties and general applicability. However, there are some reports for the preparation of highly luminescent green-emitting CDs (G-CDs), although these reports seem not to be extensible. Herein, new G-CDs (quantum yield: 27.2%) were synthesized from a facile hydrothermal treatment of p-aminosalicylic acid and ethylene glycol dimethacrylate as both carbon and nitrogen source and cross-linking agent, respectively. The chemical composition and optical properties of the as-prepared G-CDs were successfully investigated using transmission electron microscopy, atomic force microscopy, dynamic light scattering, X-ray diffraction, energy dispersive X-ray spectroscopy, Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy and fluorescence and UV–vis spectroscopies. Interestingly, the fluorescence intensity of G-CDs was selectivity quenched by Fe3+ in the range of 0.05–10.0 µmol Lˉ1, with a detection limit of 13.7 nmol Lˉ1. Meanwhile, ascorbic acid found to reduce Fe3+ to Fe2+, thereby causing restoration of the fluorescence of G-CDs. The detection limit for ascorbic acid detection was estimated as 82.0 nmol Lˉ1 over a linear range from 0.2 to 11.0 µmol Lˉ1. Furthermore, the designed sensing platform was successfully utilized to the detection of Fe3+ and ascorbic acid in water and urine samples and to intracellular imaging without surface modification.

ce:section.ce:para: Luminescent carbon dots (CDs) have attracted growing interest in the last few years because of their fascinating physicochemical properties in biosensors [1–3], optoelectronic devices [4], drug delivery [5,6], light-emitting devices [7,8] and so on. High photoluminescence quantum yields (PL QYs), chemical and physical stability, superior water solubility, low toxicity, and excellent biocompatibility are the most interesting properties of CDs [9]. The simplicity in the preparation routes, facility in surface modification, and low cost of precursors are the other properties of CDs [9]. In most studies, blue-emitting CDs (B-CDs) have been prepared through different methods and precursors exhibiting an intense emission with maxima in the violet or blue region [10–12], which is not preferred for bio-imaging due to the autofluorescence of the cells in blue spectral region. Moreover, the low visible absorption of CDs may restrict their applications in solar cells and light emitting devices [13]. In contrast, CDs with a longer fluorescence emission (i.e. green, yellow or red emission) and a higher PL QY would be better for many applications, especially in bio-imaging and optoelectronic devices. Accordingly, the development of routes to extend the fluorescence emission of CDs into longer wavelength regions with high PL QY is of great importance in current research focus on the preparation of CDs [14–18]. CDs with longer wavelength emissions are generally prepared by the selection of proper precursors containing aromatic rings and several heteroatoms under the controlled synthesis conditions including, pH, temperature and time [18]. In addition, the crosslinking of CDs could be performed to make sure the intense emission with longer wavelength and structural stability [19,20].

To date, there have been some systematic reports on the synthesis of green-emitting CDs (G-CDs). Wang et al. synthesized G-CDs through heat treatment of phytic acid and ethylenediamine in ultra-water with the average diameter of 9 nm [21]. This average diameter was larger than most other luminescent B-CDs. On the base of these observations, the green emission of CDs can be attributed to the quantum size effects. Similarly, Tian et al. synthesized full-color emissive CDs by employing three different solvents, in which red-shifted absorption and emission of CDs was ascribed to the gradually increasing overall particle size [22]. Wang et al. prepared highly fluorescent water-soluble G-CDs by molecular fusion of 1,3,6-trinitropyrene through heat treatment in alkaline medium, where alkaline species such as OH−, NH3 and NH2NH2 revealed a critical function in optical properties, surface functionalization, and tuning the size of CDs [13]. Meanwhile, Ding et al. prepared full-color light-emitting CDs by hydrothermal treatment of urea and p-phenylenediamine [16]. They found that the prepared CDs possess similar distributions of particle size, while the degree of surface oxidation tuned the color emission of CDs. Although the origin of the luminescence in CDs has been ascribed to the quantum size effects and surface defect states, the exact luminescence cause of CDs is still controversial and even contradictory. Hence, more studies are needed to obtain deep insight into the fluorescence mechanism of CDs and the synthesis of luminescent CDs with longer wavelength.

Detection of Fe3+ in aqueous sample is still of great importance because Fe3+ ions play a vital role in biological systems, via binding with different regulatory proteins and is one of the most important metals in environment [23]. To date, a number of works have been reported for the detection of Fe3+ based on CDs. For example, Zhou et al. synthesized CDs via a facile hydrothermal procedure using citric acid and Tris as precursors [24]. They found that the CDs exhibit a high affinity for Fe3+ ions with a good linear correlation (R2= 0.997) over the concentration range from 2 to 50 µmol Lˉ1. Lu's research group prepared carbon nanodots with blue emission for the detection of Fe3+ ions over the concentration range of 0–7.16 mmol L ˉ1 and cell imaging in HL-60 cells [25]. Qu and his coworkers synthesized carbon nanoparticles as a very effective fluorescent sensing platform for sensitive detection of Fe3+ ions with a detection limit of 0.32 µmol Lˉ1 [26]. However, it has been remained a wish to construct a fluorescent nanoprobe based on CDs that possess the high PL QY, good biocompatibility and sensitivity for Fe3+ concentrations in aqueous samples and living cells.

Herein, we report a simple and general route to the synthesis of G-CDs with high PL QY through the hydrothermal treatment of an organic precursor p-aminosalicylic acid (PAS) containing aromatic ring and amino group and a cross-linking agent ethyleneglycol dimethacrylate (EGDMA). The role of the cross-linking agent is crucial because of linking the carbonic core in carbonization process and providing higher probability of sp2 cluster structure [27], which leads to an increase in size and formation of the emissive traps on the edges of the resulting CDs and extending the fluorescence emission of the prepared CDs to longer wavelength regions. G-CDs with PL QY of 27.2% were readily synthesized by hydrothermal treatment of PAS and EGDMA as a model precursor widely used in polymer chemistry. Then, the as-prepared G-CDs were used for highly sensitive and selective detection of Fe3+ and ascorbic acid ( Scheme 1). Further, the G-CDs were evaluated as fluorescent nanoplatforms for the intracellular imaging and sensing of Fe3+ in living cells and real water samples, respectively.

PAS was purchased from Sigma-Aldrich. EGDMA, sodium citrate, glucose, ascorbic acid, cysteine, histidine, alanine, tyrosine, glycine, and serine were purchased from Merck. All inorganic salts were purchased from Merck. The stock solutions were prepared with double distilled water.

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) photographs were obtained from a Zeiss EM 900 transmission electron microscope operating at 150 kV. Dynamic light scattering (DLS) and Zeta potential were performed by using a Malvern Instrument Zetasizer, Atomic force microscopy (AFM) images were recorded using a Veeco CP Research instrument with a Si cantilever in the contact mode. A dilute aqueous solution of G-CDs was deposited onto a freshly cleaved mica substrate in order to prepare a sample for AFM analysis. The X-ray powder diffraction (XRD) pattern was measured on a Philips-PW 12 C diffractometer using Cu Kα radiation (λ = 1.541 Å). Energy dispersive X-ray (EDX) spectrum was recorded with a Phenom ProX scanning electron microscope equipped with EDX to identify the composition of G-CDs. FT-IR spectra in KBr pellet were taken on a Bruker IFS-66 FT-IR spectrometer (400–4000 cm−1). Fluorescence lifetimes were obtained with an Edinburgh Instrument FLS920 equipped with a xenon lamp as the excitation source (excitation wavelength 365 nm). All fluorescence measurements were carried out on a Perkin-Elmer LS-50B fluorescence spectrometer equipped with a xenon flash lamp. The absorbance spectra were recorded on a Model Scinco UV S-2100.

Typically, the G-CDs were prepared by hydrothermal method using PAS as both carbon and nitrogen source and EGDMA as across-linker agent. Briefly, 300 mg PAS and 500 µL EGDMA were dissolved in 30 mL double distilled water. The solution pH was adjusted to 12 by adding 1 mol Lˉ1 NaOH. The resulting solution was transferred into a Teflon-lined stainless steel autoclave and heated at 180 °C for 10 h. Then, the autoclave was cooled to room temperature and a deep yellow solution with dark brown precipitate was resulted. The solution was filtered through a 0.22 µm filter membrane to remove any insoluble particles. The resultant supernatant was dialyzed in a dialysis bag (molecular weight cut-off of 3.5 kDa) for 3 days to remove the impurities such as precursor molecules and inorganic ions.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence detection of Fe3+ and ascorbic acid
ce:section.ce:para: The quenching effect of Fe3+ on fluorescence intensity of G-CDs was conducted as follows. Firstly, a volume of 2.5 µL of G-CDs solution (1 mg mL−1) was diluted to 1.0 mL with phosphate-buffered saline (PBS) (10 mmol Lˉ1, pH 5), and the fluorescence intensity at 520 nm was measured. Then, the solution was titrated by successive additions of Fe3+ (0–50 µmol Lˉ1). After each addition of Fe3+, the solution was thoroughly mixed. Finally, the fluorescence intensity was recorded after 2 min at room temperature. All the fluorescence spectra were obtained over the wavelength range of 450 − 670 nm at an excitation wavelength of 390 nm.

The process of ascorbic acid detection was performed as follows. Firstly, Fe3+ with a final concentration of 10 µmol Lˉ1 was added to 1.0 mL of G-CDs (2.5 µg mL−1) to form a sensitive fluorescent sensing platform, which was denoted as G-CDs/Fe3+. Then, various volumes of ascorbic acid (1.0 mmol Lˉ1) were consecutively added to G-CDs/Fe3+. The final concentration of ascorbic acid varied from 0.0 to 30.0 µmol Lˉ1. After incubation for 15 min, the fluorescence spectra were monitored at 520 nm under excitation at 390 nm.

River water and well water samples were collected from the north of Tehran, Iran. The bottled mineral water was obtained from a local supermarket. Before analysis, the collected samples were filtered through a cellulose membrane filter (Millipore, 0.45 µm) to eliminate the suspended solids, and then diluted 20 times by ultrapure water. The diluted samples were adjusted to pH 5.0, and stored in refrigerator. The standard addition method was used to estimate the concentration of Fe3+, in the real samples in order to reduce any matrix effect. For the determination of Fe3+, G-CDs was added to 1.0 mL of each real samples to acquire a concentration of 2.5 µg mL−1, under well-mixing conditions, and the samples were then spiked with standard solution of Fe3+ (1.0 mmol Lˉ1). Subsequently, the fluorescence emission spectra were collected at an excitation wavelength of 390 nm.

The practical utility of the G-CDs/Fe3+-based sensing platform for detecting ascorbic acid was evaluated by the analysis of human urine samples collected from healthy volunteers (from 24 to 30 years of age). The urine samples were diluted by only 50-fold with PBS, without any further sample preparation.

The cell viability experiment was carried out using MTT assay. Typically, BT474 cells were seeded into a 96-well cell culture plate in Dulbecco's Modified Eagle's medium (DMEM) supplemented with 10% FBS at a density of 104 cells per well and incubated at 37 °C for 24 h under 5% CO2. Thereafter, the cells were incubated with medium containing different concentrations of G-CDs for 24 h at 37 °C in humidified incubator with 5% CO2 atmosphere. After the end of incubation, the cells were washed once with PBS buffer (20 mL, pH 7.4) and then incubated with MTT salt solution (5 mg mL−1 in PBS) for 5 h. Subsequently, the medium was aspirated and 100 µL of dimethylsulfoxide (DMSO) was added to wells to dissolve the formazan crystals. The absorbance of each well was measured on a microplate reader (ELx800, Biotek, USA) at 570 nm. In addition, the control experiments were conducted in the absence of G-CDs.

The BT474 cells were first cultured in DMEM medium supplemented with 10% FBS and 5% CO2 at 37 °C overnight followed by incubation with 0.5 mg mL−1 G-CDs for 6 h. After incubation, the supernatant was removed and cells were washed three times with PBS buffer to remove the free G-CDs. Finally, the cellular imaging of the treated cells was performed on a Cytation™ 3 Cell Imaging Multi-Mode Reader (BioTek Instruments, Winooski, VT, USA). In order to assess Fe3+ uptake, the BT474 cells were first incubated with 0.5 mg mL−1 G-CDs for 6 h as aforementioned above, then incubated with 0.8 µmol Lˉ1 FeCl3 for 1 h at 37 °C.

The G-CDs with high PL QY were prepared by one-step hydrothermal treatment of PAS and EGDMA in aqueous solution. PAS was chosen because it has an aromatic ring, an amino group in para position and oxygenous carbon, which all have a vital role to the synthesis of high-quality CDs. The amino group acts as a nitrogen dopant, enhancing the quantum yield, and also it is a nucleophilic group which can attack the carbon of carbonyl moieties, producing C˭N and C-N groups in the surface of CDs, which enhances the fluorescence efficiency significantly [28]. In addition, in the presence of EGDMA the amino groups can cause an increase in the size of CDs, resulting in the synthesis of CDs with longer wavelength emission based on the quantum size effect [29]. Jiang at al. reported that red, green, and blue emitting-CDs could be easily prepared by using the three different phenylenediamine isomers through solvothermal method, in which the para isomer is especially convenient for the synthesis of CDs with larger size than the other isomers [14].

Various sets of precursors and cross-linkers as well as different solvents were applied for the synthesis of CDs at the same hydrothermal reaction to show the function of amino group and cross-linker agent and the extensibility of our method (Table S1). CDs with blue emission were obtained when PAS was used as the precursor without using any cross-linker (Fig. S1a). While, G-CDs were readily prepared when PAS was used as carbon precursor in the presence of a cross-linker (EGDMA or ascorbic acid), revealing the crucial role of cross-linker agent in the synthesis of G-CDs (Fig. S1b and c). In the other hand, using sodium citrate and glucose, two widely used carbon sources in the synthesis of CDs, resulted in blue emissive CDs in the presence or absence of the cross-linker which could be attributed to the lack of amine groups (Fig. S1d-g). As can be seen from Table S1, the G-CDs were only produced when the compounds containing amino group and the cross-linker agent were existed together. It should be noted that the hydrothermal treatment of PAS/EGDMA in dimethyl formamide as an organic solvent also produces G-CDs (Fig. S1h), but their PL QY was lower than those produced from aqueous solutions. Finally, for further experiments, we selected G-CDs produced from PAS and EGDMA in aqueous because of their green fluorescence with high PL QYs.

Meanwhile, to synthesis G-CDs with maximum fluorescence intensity, in the course of hydrothermal procedure, we explored the influence of various parameters including the reaction temperature (160, 180, 200 and 220 °C) and reaction time (2, 6, 8, 10, 16 and 18 h), and the resulting data are shown in Fig. S2. As it is obvious from Fig. S2, a deep yellow solution with highest fluorescence intensity was achieved for an aqueous solution containing 300 mg PAS and 500 µL EGDMA reacted at a temperature of 180 °C and a time period of 10 h. Therefore, 180 °C and 10 h were selected as the optimal conditions for the synthesis of G-CDs. The size and morphology of G-CDs were characterized by TEM, AFM and DLS measurements. As shown in Fig. 1a1, the TEM image shows that G-CDs had spherical shape and non-aggregation characteristics with an average diameter about of 8 nm. The average diameter is clearly larger than the diameter of the most of the reported luminescent CDs with blue emission, indicating the green emission is originated from the size effect [21]. To further confirm this hypothesis, the TEM image of blue emitting-CDs synthesized from PAS without using cross-linker was taken. It can be seen from Fig. 1a2 the B-CDs had a spherical shape with an average diameter about 3 nm.

The AFM image clearly illustrated that the heights of G-CDs were in the range of 1.2–1.8 nm, with an average of ca. 1.6 nm (Fig. S3a). The aggregations observed in the AFM image might be formed during the sample preparation for AFM testing [30]. The DLS results indicated that the average hydrodynamic diameter is 11.7 nm, as shown in Fig. S3b. This value is higher than the size obtained by TEM and AFM measurements, due to the fact that DLS determines the overall hydrodynamic particle size of the involved particles, ions and adsorbed molecules. The zeta potential of G-CDs measured at pH 7.0 was found to be − 15.25 mV (Fig. S3c), which is due to the presence of carboxylic and hydroxyl groups at their surfaces. The XRD patterns of G-CDs, shown in Fig. S4, demonstrated a large broad peak centered at 2θ= 28.6 Å, and a weak peak at 2θ= 41.4 Å, that are corresponded to (002) and (101) diffraction patterns of graphitic carbon, respectively [31]. The interlayer spacing (d) of G-CDs (3.15 nm) is higher than that of the graphitic interlayer spacing (0.34 nm), indicating the poor crystalline nature of G-CDs, which is attributed to the introduction of more oxygen and nitrogen containing groups [32].

Then, the energy-dispersed spectroscopy (EDS) and FT-IR were used to characterize the chemical composition and functionalization of G-CDs. The chemical composition of G-CDs was obtained by collecting the corresponding EDS results (Fig. S5). The elemental analysis was as follows: C 50.6%, O 33.0%, N 10.6% and Si 5.7%. The high percentage of O in G-CDs observed by EDS measurement, was further confirmed the XRD results. The existence of peak of N atoms indicates the successful doping of N atoms into G-CDs. On the other hand, the peak of Si atoms is attributed to the glass slide used for EDS analysis. As shown in Fig. 1b, the peaks at around 1615.5, 1020.3, 1263.3 and 802.4 cm−1 could be attributed to the stretching vibrations C˭O, C-O, C-O-C and aromatic Ar C-H bonds, respectively, indicating the oxygen-rich property of G-CDs [33]. The broad and intense peaks located at 3077.8–3610.1 cm−1 corresponds to the stretching modes of O-H and N-H groups. The peak located at 1388.4 cm−1 may be ascribed to the C-N bond, while the peak located at 1420.5 cm−1 could be ascribed to the C˭N bond [34], which were aroused from interaction between PAS and EGDMA. Based on the above FT-IR analysis, the existence of different hydrophilic functional groups on G-CDs surface can be resulted in excellent water solubility and great potential for sensing in aqueous samples.

The optical properties, including absorption, excitation and emission spectra, and PL QY of the as-prepared G-CDs were evaluated. The absorption spectrum of G-CDs aqueous solution shows three characteristic peaks at 235, 335 and 400 nm ( Fig. 2a), with a tail extending into the visible range. The peak located at 235 nm may be ascribed to π–π * transition of the C=C bond, while the peak located at 335 nm could be ascribed to the n–π * transition of the C=O and C=N bonds, which cannot induce noticeable fluorescence signal [35]. The peak at 400 nm could be assigned to the n–π transition of the carboxyl and/or C−N bond, which leads to a strong green emission [15]. Therefore, based on the TEM and UV–vis results, the origin of green emission of the prepared CDs can be attributed to both quantum size effects and surface defect states.

As is obvious from Fig. 2a, the G-CDs showed strong emission band centered at approximately λ = 520 nm, when excited at 390 nm. Meanwhile, the aqueous solution of G-CDs exhibits a strong green emission under UV light of 365 nm. To further study the optical properties of G-CDs, the emission spectra of G-CDs were measured at various excitation wavelengths ranging from 340 to 430 nm, and the result is shown in Fig. 2b. As seen, the emission intensity of G-CDs increases with increasing in excitation wavelengths from 340 to 390 nm, and after that, decreases gradually with a further increase in excitation wavelength, while the fluorescence emission peak at 520 nm remains unchanged, which indicates the excitation-independent fluorescence behavior of G-CDs. This behavior is attributed to more uniform size and surface defects of G-CDs, in agreement with TEM, AFM and DLS measurements [35]. Using fluorescein (with PL QY 0.79 in 0.1 M NaOH) as the reference, the relative PLQY of G-CDs in aqueous solution was calculated to be 27.2% at an excitation wavelength of 390 nm, which is either comparable or even improved, compared to the previous reports (Table S2). Such high PL QY can be ascribed to the presence of hetero atom N, introducing a new type of surface state (C-N and C=N). Moreover, the calculated PL QY is a satisfactory for fluorescent probes to be used as photoluminescence sensing platform for the detection of metal ions [36].

The photoluminescence intensity of synthesized G-CDs at the maximum emission (520 nm) was then recorded to evaluate their photostability under continuous illumination at 390 nm and long-term storage stability under ambient conditions. As illustrated in Fig. S6, there was insignificant change in fluorescence intensity after 60 min of continuous illumination at 390 nm and 45 days at ambient conditions, clearly revealing the high stability of G-CDs. Such a high photostability of G-CDs is a key issue for their application as bio-imaging and bio-labeling applications. The fluorescence intensities of G-CDs remained nearly constant at a pH range of 4–12, as shown in Fig. 2c. Further, the influences of different common solvents on the fluorescence intensity of G-CDs were explored. Fig. 3d shows a solvatochromic emission with a blue-shift upon decreasing solvent polarity revealing that G-CDs possess different energy gaps in different solvents [37,38]. Inset of Fig. 2b illustrates the images of different solvents before (top) and after (bottom) addition of G-CDs, taken under UV light (365 nm).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Use of fluorescent G-CDs nanoprobes for detection of Fe3+ ions and ascorbic acid
ce:section.ce:para: It was expected that G-CDs could act as a fluorescent nanoprobe for detection of Fe3+, due to the existence of abundant oxygen-containing and amino functional groups on their surfaces [39–41], as confirmed by FT-IR, XRD and EDX analysis. Therefore, we investigated the possibility of G-CDs for fluorescence detection of Fe3+. Fig. 3a illustrates the fluorescence emission spectra of G-CDs aqueous solution after adding different concentrations of Fe3+. As is obvious, a gradual decrease in fluorescence signal at 520 nm was observed upon addition of Fe3+ ions, so that the G-CDs could be used as fluorescence probe for the detection of Fe3+. The Zeta potential measurements were performed to elucidate whether Fe3+ coordinates with oxygen containing or amino groups of the G-CDs surface. As is shown in Fig. S7, the zeta potential value was varied from − 15.25 mV to − 23.6 mV after addition of Fe3+, which indicated that the amino groups on the G-CDs surface are coordinated with Fe3+ and their positive charge is somewhat shielded.

To achieve the best sensing performance for Fe3+ detection, the effects of incubation time and solution pH were evaluated and optimized. It was observed that the fluorescence intensity of G-CDs decreased immediately after addition of Fe3+ (1.0 µmol Lˉ1), and then slightly decreased (Fig. S8). Therefore, an incubation time of 2 min was selected as the optimal time for further investigation. It is well-known that the surface functionality of CDs is significantly pH-dependent and, thus, the pH has an essential role in Fe3+ detection by G-CDs. Fig. S9 depicts the fluorescence intensity of G-CDs at various pH values before and after the addition of Fe3+ (0.5 µmol Lˉ1). As seen, the fluorescence intensity decreases significantly at pH 5 after Fe3+ addition. This implies that the interaction between Fe3+ and amino groups in this condition is stronger than that at other pHs. Therefore, we selected a pH of 5 as the optimum pH for further experiments. This result obviously demonstrates that the coordination of amino at the surface of G-CDs plays critical role in enhancing the interaction with Fe3+, which then resulted in the fluorescence quenching of G-CDs. Under optimal conditions, the dependence of F0/F on the Fe3+ concentration was studied and the results are shown in Fig. 3b, in which F0 and F are the fluorescence intensities at 520 nm before and after the addition of Fe3+, respectively. The linear response for Fe3+ was obtained in the ranges of 0.05–10.00 µmol Lˉ1 with a correlation coefficient of 0.992, implying a satisfactory sensing performance in detection of traces of Fe3+. The limit of detection (LOD) was estimated to be 13.7 nmol Lˉ1, based on a 3δ/m, where δ is the standard deviation of the blank signal and m is the slope of the linear calibration curve. This LOD is much lower than the maximum level (0.54 µmol Lˉ1) of Fe3+ allowed in drinking water by the U.S. Environmental Protection Agency.

Table 1 compares the linear range and LOD of this sensing platform with a number of different fluorescent sensing platforms for Fe3+ detection [41–47]. As seen, the linear range of the developed sensing platform is either better than or at least comparable with that of the previously reported sensing platforms. In addition, the LOD obtained in present work is improved over that of most reported platforms listed in Table 1. It is worth noting that the proposed method did not require expensive instrument and materials, modification processes, and harsh synthesis conditions.

The selectivity of the fluorescent nanoprobe for Fe3+ (0.5 µmol Lˉ1) was also conducted by testing the response of the proposed sensing platform against other various metal ions (50 µmol Lˉ1). Fig. S10 and Fig. 3c show that the addition of Na+, Ca2+, Ba2+, Cu2+ Fe2+, Hg2+, Zn2+, Co2+, Mn2+, Ag+, Ce3+, Al3+ and Cr3+ did not have noticeable effects on fluorescence emission of G-CDs. The obtained results clearly imply that the G-CDs have a great selectivity and sensitivity towards Fe3+, which can be promising for practical applications.

Given the high selectivity of G-CDs for Fe3+ ions in comparison to Fe2+ ions, a general sensing platform could be established for the iron reductant detection. Hence, ascorbic acid was selected as a proof-of-concept. It is well known that Fe3+ can be reduced to Fe2+ by ascorbic acid, which itself has no measurable quenching effect on the fluorescence intensity of G-CDs. In fact, the quenched fluorescence of G-CDs/Fe3+ probe can be nicely regained upon addition of ascorbic acid. Thus, the G-CDs/Fe3+ sensing platform could be utilized as a “turn-on” fluorescent system for ascorbic acid detection. It should be noted that no significant influence on the fluorescence intensity of G-CDs was observed in the presence of ascorbic acid itself (Fig. S11).

The response time of G-CDs/Fe3+ towards ascorbic acid in the fluorescence emission intensity was measured at 520 nm, as can be seen in Fig. S12. After addition of ascorbic acid, the fluorescence intensity was increased gradually and reached a maximum after 15 min, which was considered as the optimized reaction time. As can be seen from Fig. 3d and e the fluorescence intensity of the G-CDs/Fe3+ was linearly increased with the ascorbic acid concentration from 0.2 to 11.0 µmol Lˉ1 with a correlation coefficient of R2= 0.990. The limit of detection for ascorbic acid on the G-CDs/Fe3+ probe was found to be 82.0 nmol Lˉ1 at a signal-to-noise ratio of 3.

Moreover, Table 1 is also compares the linear range and limit of detection of the proposed fluorescent method with those of the previously published methods based on different fluorescence nanostructures for detection of ascorbic acid [48–53]. The results indicated that the linear range and limit of detection of the proposed method is among the beat methods reported for the ascorbic acid determination. To evaluate the selectivity of the G-CDs/Fe3+ for ascorbic acid detection (10 µmol Lˉ1), we explored the interference of common metal ions (100 µmol Lˉ1 of Na+, K+, Ca2+, Mg2+) and amino acids (100 µmol Lˉ1 of cysteine, histidine, alanine, tyrosine, glycine, serine) on the sensing system and the results are shown in Fig. 3f. As is quite obvious, the interference experiments showed that the most metal ions and amino acids have no noticeable effect on the fluorescence intensity of the G-CDs/Fe3+, revealing that this sensing platform has a high selectivity towards ascorbic acid.

Generally, the fluorescence quenching of CDs originates from static quenching (i.e., the formation of a non-photoluminescent complex) or dynamic quenching (i.e., electron transfer processes). The fluorescent quenching of G-CDs by Fe3+ ions could be attributed to the effective coordination or chelation interactions between Fe3+ ions and various functional groups on the surface of G-CDs [24]. These strong interactions facilitate the partial transfer of an electron/charge in the excited state of G-CDs to the d orbital of Fe3+ and resulting in the substantial fluorescence quenching. The fluorescence lifetime spectroscopy has been usually used to distinguish between static and dynamic quenching pathways. Here, we investigated the fluorescence lifetime of G-CDs in the absence and presence of Fe3+, and the resulting spectra were given in Fig. 4a. The average lifetime of G-CDs was 7.82 ns, clearly indicating an ultrafast exciton recombination process. In addition, the average life time of G-CDs was nearly constant (7.79 ns) after coordination with Fe3+. In order to further understand the fluorescence quenching mechanism, the fluorescence-base Stern-Volmer plot of G-CDs after addition of Fe3+ were also evaluated as:
F
0
/ F = 1 +
K
s v
[ Q ]
where F0 and F were the fluorescence intensity of G-CDs at 520 nm in absence and presence of Fe3+, respectively, [Q] is the concentration of the quencher (Fe3+), and Ksv is the static Stern-Volmer constant. The establishment of the Stern-Volmer equation represented that they followed a linear range with a Ksv = 8.7 × 104 mol ˉ1 L. Judging from the constant fluorescence lifetime of 7.82 ns and relatively large magnitude of Ksv, the fluorescence quenching of G-CDs by Fe3+ is most probably owing to a static quenching mechanism arising from the formation of a quite stable complex.

To further confirm the quenching type, the UV–vis spectroscopy was used to study the fluorescence quenching and restoration mechanism of the sensing platform. As can be seen from Fig. S13, upon addition of Fe3+to the prepared G-CDs solution, an apparent decrease of the absorption peak centered at 400 nm takes place, while the absorption peak at 335 in the UV–vis spectrum nm is increased. Moreover, the absorption peaks of G-CDs after adding of Fe3+ shifted to longer wavelengths, which indicate that Fe3+ is coordinated with G-CDs and, hence, the fluorescence quenching of G-CDs by Fe3+ is presumably related to static quenching originating from the formation of a non-fluorescence complex between Fe3+ and functional groups on the G-CDs surface [1]. As can also be seen from Fig. S13, an apparent decrease of the absorption peak of G-CDs/Fe3+ at 350 nm was observed after addition of ascorbic acid. This observation revealed that the restoration of the fluorescence could be ascribed to the redox reaction between Fe3+ and ascorbic acid, in which Fe3+ has been reduced to Fe2+ by ascorbic acid.

In order to demonstrate the feasibility of the proposed method for practical applications, it was applied to the determination Fe3+ ion in well, river and bottled mineral waters. The standard addition method was used to determine the unknown concentration of Fe3+ in the water samples. The obtained results are presented in Table 2. Further, the recovery assays for different concentrations of Fe3+ were performed. As it can be seen from Table 2, the recoveries varied in the acceptable range of 94.0–108.0% with the relative standard deviation (RSD) variation from 1.45% to 4.22%, indicating the validity of the method. In addition, the results of determination of Fe3+ ions in real samples were compared with those obtained from the ICP-OES (Vista-Pro, Varian, USA) method. The statistical analysis of experimental data was assessed using the Student's t-test (the t-test value is 4.3 at a 95% confidence level). According to statistical analysis by Student t-test (Table 2), the detection results agreed well with those obtained from the ICP-OES method. These results indicated that, despite the presence of numerous mineral and biological compounds in the water samples, G-CDs could effectively distinguish between Fe3+ and other compounds and well demonstrated the feasibility and reliability of G-CDs sensing platform for quantifying Fe3+ in real samples.

The potential application of the G-CDs/Fe3+ sensing platform for detecting ascorbic acid in human urine samples was also investigated. The urine samples were subjected to a 50-fold dilution to reduce the inference from urine samples and spiked with different concentrations of ascorbic acid added. The analytical results summarized in Table 2 showed that the recoveries varied from 90% to 102% with RSD values lower than 4.9%, suggesting satisfactory recoveries in the real sample with the acceptable analytical precision. To validate the sensor's accuracy, ascorbic acid in real sample was also determined using a HPLC-UV method [54]. According on a t-test (the Student's t value was 4.3 at p = 0.05), the two methods did not provide significantly different results (Table 2). These results revealed the reliability of the G-CDs/Fe3+-based sensing platform for the determination of ascorbic acid in biological samples.

In the next step, we evaluated the applicability of G-CDs as fluorescent nanoprobes for cellular imaging. The cytotoxic effect of G-CDs on BT474 cells was first investigated using MTT assay to show their potential in the bio-applications [55]. MTT assay was carried out for BT474 cells with various concentrations of G-CDs (i.e., 0.01, 0.05, 0.1, 0.5, 1.0, and 1.5 mg mL−1). As illustrated in Fig. 4a, almost 85% of BT474 cells remained alive even after cultivation with a high concentration of G-CDs (i.e., 1.5 mg mL–1) for 24 h. This result reveals that G-CDs has great biocompatibility and very low cytotoxicity with high levels about 1.5 mg mL–1. Hence, G-CDs could hold great promise as an ideal intracellular probe for Fe3+ because of their remarkable optical properties and low cytotoxicity. To elucidate the feasibility of G-CDs for cellular imaging, fluorescent bioimaging of intracellular Fe3+ were conducted subsequently using a fluorescent microscopy in BT474 cells. Fig. 4b illustrates the treatment of cells with G-CDs (0.5 mg mL−1) for 6 h. Upon a laser excitation at 488 nm, the BT474 cells emitted bright green fluorescence from the intracellular region, which clearly indicates the occurrence of cellular uptake of G-CDs. In addition, any change on the fluorescence properties of G-CDs were not observed, reflecting the high stability of G-CDs in cell culture medium. The bright field images of the BT474 cells incubated with G-CDs and G-CDs/Fe3+ (Fig. 4c and f) showed the normal morphology of the cells, confirming that G-CDs are biocompatible and possess minimum cytotoxicity. Encouraged by low cytotoxicity and superior biocompatibility, the G-CDs with distinctive green luminescence were used for semi-quantitative intracellular imaging of Fe3+ in living cells. As expected, the intracellular fluorescence of BT474 cells decreased after addition of exogenous Fe3+ into the G-CDs-pretreated BT474 cells (Fig. 4e), which indicated this nanoprobe could be used for intracellular imaging of Fe3+.

In summary, we successfully prepared and fully characterized new green carbon dots (G-CDs) with high PL QY of 27.2%, using p-aminosalicylic acid (PAS) and ethyleneglycol dimethacrylate (EGDMA) as precursors via a facile one-pot hydrothermal method. The basic idea of our method was the use of PAS containing aromatic ring and amino group and a crosslinking agent EGDMA, which increased the size and promoted the formation of C-N bonds on the surface of G-CDs. The results showed that G-CDs can be easily prepared by using such suitable procures and cross-linkers. The synthesized G-CDs were successfully used as sensitive and selective nanoprobes for detection of low levels of Fe3+ ion and ascorbic acid, via an off-on process. The G-CDs were also used as fluorescent nanoprobes for cellular imaging due to their excellent biocompatibility and small size. Finally, this study has opened an avenue to the synthesis of green emitting carbon dots with high quantum yield, which can also be applied as sensitive platforms for different applications such as sensing and bioimaging without the need for further surface modification. Therefore, the G-CDs are promising fluorescent imaging agents with advantages including their facile and large scale synthesis, low toxicity, physical and chemical stability for different applications, especially for intracellular imaging.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors gratefully acknowledge the support of this work by the Research Councils of Tarbiat Modares and Razi Universities, Iran Science Elites Federation (ISEF) and Iran National Science Foundation (INSF).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.talanta.2018.02.042.
